text: The surgeon encounters dense adhesions of the small bowel plastered over the pelvis, obscuring the view of the pelvic structures due to the extensive adhesion formation.
The surgeon navigates along the right iliac region, encountering a loop of small bowel that is adhered to surrounding structures.
The surgeon's left hand is positioned above a loop of small bowel that is adhered to the bladder on the right side, demonstrating the plastered adhesions in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies another loop of small bowel directly behind the previously mentioned loop, adjacent to the left dome of the bladder. The colon is visible nearby.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the small bowel to expose the pelvic region, avoiding injury. A cavity is visible, with its rind clearly delineated.
The surgeon maneuvers a loop of bowel to expose the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon, revealing additional loops of small bowel that require further dissection.
The surgeon observes pus emerging from interloop abscesses that were not drained by the IR drain, causing surrounding tissues to be drawn inward.
The surgeon carefully separates a loop of bowel from the surrounding fibrous rind, with the pelvis still not visible, indicating the complexity of the resection.
The surgeon performs cold scissors dissection and blunt pushing to separate tissue from the bladder flap, using minimal cautery.

The surgeon separates a loop of small bowel from the rectus sigmoid mesentery at the pelvic brim, revealing the uterus below. The loop of small bowel is lifted, exposing the area where the majority of the abscess cavity was located, with some residual pus visible.
The surgeon lifts the colon to reveal the deeper pelvis, identifying the right ureter. The colon is elevated, and the surgeon works to remove the remaining small bowel from the area.
The surgeon identifies the cecum, appendix, and terminal ileum, emphasizing the importance of freeing these structures, especially if an ileostomy is chosen.

The surgeon identifies the cecum, appendix, and terminal ileum, and proceeds to mobilize the terminal ileum mesentery. The small bowel is fully mobilized, allowing the surgeon to visually assess the pelvis.

A loop of sigmoid colon is visible, flopped down into the deep pelvis. The colon appears adhered to the left dome of the bladder. The surgeon has not yet reached the left aspect of the pelvis or located the IR drain.
The surgeon uses the robotic arm to separate the colon from the uterus, revealing the anterior pelvis while carefully preserving the left fallopian tube and ovary.
The surgeon identifies the IR drain entering from the posterior left pelvis, observing the cleaned-up abscess area. The uterus is suspended to enhance access and visualization of the pelvic region.
The surgeon navigates into the deep anterior pelvis to identify the loop of sigmoid colon flopped over the area, aiming to locate the true rectum.

The surgeon manipulates the right lateral mesorectum, which is adherent to the anterior portion of the colon. Suction is used to clear blood from the surgical field. The procedure is performed using four ports, with port number one located in the left upper quadrant.

The surgeon utilizes four ports for the procedure: port two in the epigastrium, port three in the right mid quadrant where the ileostomy would have been, and port four in the right lower quadrant. The camera is positioned in arm three, allowing the surgeon to operate with two left hands.

In the deeper pelvis, the surgeon uses scissors in arm number four, which occasionally contact the iliac vessels. The surgeon adjusts the camera and scissors positions to avoid obstruction, encountering a portion of tissue that is adherent and challenging to dissect.

The surgeon uses a scope to check for the anterior reflection but finds a loop of colon flopped over the cul-de-sac, which has not yet been identified. The surgeon continues to work to reach this area.

The surgeon identifies the left lateral border of the pelvic dissection using the left ureter as a landmark. The dissection progresses as the surgeon follows the ureter down into the pelvis, revealing a small hole in the mesentery.
The surgeon locates the left ureter, which serves as a landmark for the left lateral border of the pelvic dissection.
The surgeon moves the rectosigmoid colon off the sidewall, following a potentially unviolated plane down into the pelvis, using a lateral approach.

The surgeon lifts the mesorectum posteriorly to join the dissection medially, following down the right side of the pelvic dissection. This maneuver provides access to the deep pelvis in a proper plane and facilitates manipulation of the bowel that is adhered in the area.

The surgeon observes the colon adhered to the right lateral mesorectum and proceeds to dissect through the tissue with precision, ensuring not to damage the anterior wall of the rectum. The dissection reveals a clear plane, allowing for improved access and elevation of the colon out of the pelvis.
The surgeon separates the anterior rectum from the cul-de-sac, revealing the rectum's exposure to an abscess cavity. The tissue appears inflamed, influencing the decision to avoid an anastomosis.

The surgeon works to mobilize the remaining colon for division of the rectum, revealing the anterior wall of the rectum, which appears damaged. A quick scope is performed to confirm that no colon is flopped over the cul-de-sac.

The surgeon marks the site for the colostomy, ensuring a straight path for placement. Lateral mobilization is performed to ensure the colon reaches the marked site, followed by the removal of the pelvic drain.

The surgeon removes the pelvic drain, addressing minor bleeding by placing a sponge temporarily. The mesentery is taken, with dissection occurring closer to the bowel wall above the superior hemorrhoidal artery, followed by medial dissection beneath it.

The surgeon joins two previously dissected spots without performing a high ligation, then connects them to the bowel wall marked for a potential colostomy. The specimen is extracted through the colostomy site, eliminating the need for a new extraction incision, which will be closed during the Hartmann reversal.

The surgeon cleans the rectal mesentery, ensuring the dissection is below the loop of tissue previously flopped over the top. Some sigmoid colon is left on the rectum, with the intention for it to be resected during the Hartmann reversal.
The surgeon resects just beneath the recto-sigmoid junction due to diseased and adherent sigmoid colon, performs a perfusion assessment, and divides the upper rectum.

The surgeon creates a left upper quadrant colostomy at the end of the procedure. The camera is positioned in arm two, providing a different view as the stapler is brought in through arm three. The rectal staple line is marked with a proline suture, and a leak test is performed on the rectal stump following anterior dissection near the cul-de-sac.
